key_points: Rights are reasonable claims of individuals that are recognized by society and protected by law
Fundamental Rights are basic rights guaranteed to citizens by the Constitution of India
There are six Fundamental Rights provided in the Indian Constitution
The Right to Equality ensures that all citizens are equal before the law
The Right to Freedom provides freedoms such as speech, movement, residence and profession
The Right against Exploitation prohibits forced labour, human trafficking and child labour
The Right to Freedom of Religion allows citizens to practice and propagate their religion
Cultural and Educational Rights protect the language and culture of minority groups
The Right to Constitutional Remedies allows citizens to approach courts if their rights are violated
Democratic rights are essential for protecting freedom, equality and justice in a democracy
examples: Example of Right to Equality: Abolition of untouchability
Example of Right to Freedom: Freedom of speech and expression
Example of Right against Exploitation: Ban on child labour
Example of Cultural Rights: Minorities establishing educational institutions
Example of Constitutional Remedies: Citizens approaching the Supreme Court
Example of Religious Freedom: People practicing different religions in India
formulas: Rights → Reasonable claims recognized by society and protected by law
Fundamental Rights → Basic rights guaranteed by the Constitution
Democracy → Government where citizens enjoy freedom, equality and rights
PIL (Public Interest Litigation) → Legal action taken for public welfare
exam_tips: Remember the six Fundamental Rights mentioned in the Constitution
Dr. B. R. Ambedkar called the Right to Constitutional Remedies the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution
Right to Equality abolished untouchability in India
Right against Exploitation bans child labour and human trafficking
RTI (Right to Information) increases transparency and accountability in government
